title: Solution of a large nonlinear recurrent neural network at fixed connectivity
abstract: We calculate the moments and response functions of a nonlinear random recurrent neural network in the large $N$ limit. Our approach does not require averaging over synaptic weights and gives the first nontrivial term in a $1/\sqrt{N}$ expansion of general intensive-order correlation functions, proving a recent conjecture by Shen and Hu as a special case. Our results provide an analytical link between synaptic connectivity, correlations in spontaneous activity, and the response of a network to small perturbations.
summary: This paper provides an analytical solution for the dynamics of large nonlinear recurrent neural networks at fixed connectivity. By using a diagrammatic expansion, the author shows that the leading-order behavior is dominated by 'chain' diagrams after a specific cancellation of 'rainbow' and 'bubble' diagrams. The work proves the Shen-Hu conjecture and establishes a generalized Wick theorem for these systems. This allows for the calculation of precise correlation and response functions in the large N limit without needing to average over the random weights.
main_problem: Calculating the moments, response functions, and pairwise correlations of a large nonlinear recurrent neural network at a fixed connectivity without averaging over synaptic weights.
main_result: The paper proves a generalized Wick theorem for the network and validates the Shen-Hu conjecture, providing closed-form Fourier space equations for the linear response and correlation functions.
method: The author uses the Martin-Siggia-Rose-De Dominicis-Janssen (MSRDJ) formalism, a 1/sqrt(N) diagrammatic expansion, and graph theory (ribbon graphs and Euler's theorem) to identify and resum leading-order terms.
model_or_system: A large (N) nonlinear random recurrent neural network with dynamics defined by preactivations subject to random synaptic weights (J_ij) and Gaussian external input noise.
key_observables: Moments and response functions of pre- and post-activations, pairwise correlations, and linear response functions.
important_parameters: Large N limit, synaptic variance (sigma^2), connectivity matrix (J_ij), and the 1/sqrt(N) expansion parameter.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the large N limit, i.i.d. synaptic weights, and that the geometric series of the expansion does not diverge (sigma|phi'| < 1).
figures_summary: Figures define diagrammatic notation (gaps, bubbles, wavy lines), illustrate the difference between leading-order and subleading diagrams, and show the structure of rainbow and chain diagrams.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the model and the MSRDJ generating functional, proceeds to a diagrammatic expansion in 1/sqrt(N), uses graph theory and combinatorial arguments to prove the cancellation of certain diagrams (rainbows and bubbles), and concludes with the derivation of closed-form solutions for correlation and response functions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2. (A-B) Non-maximally disconnected diagrams. (C-D) Maximally disconnected, leading order diagrams. Note that both (B) and (C) contain four wavy lines and a set of repeated fixed indices, aµ = aν. The diagram in C contains 2 disconnected blocks, while the diagram in B contains only one.
Figure 3. (A) Bare rainbow. (B) Join and (C) nest operations applied to the bare rainbow.
Figure 4. Mean-square diagrams. We suppress the J symbols so we can label the gaps G1, · · · , G8.
Figure 5. Multigraphs for the mean-square diagrams in Fig. 4 at order k = 4. We can see that the diagram on the right has fewer vertices and is therefore subleading.
Figure 6. Ribbon graph for the top diagram in Fig. 4.

Figure 8. A rainbow and its decorations. Note that the diagram obtained by replacing only the right bare rainbow with a bubble is equivalent to the diagram in the upper right hand corner. Since we sum over equivalence classes of diagrams (Sec. 2.2), including both in the sum over decorations–i.e. counting the corresponding cρ twice–would double count the corresponding term in the expansion of Eq. (2.8)
